sectionName: Psychological, Social & Biological Foundations of Behavior

questions.passage: A developmental lab tested whether children learned a puzzle strategy more effectively from direct trial-and-error or from watching an older peer demonstrate the strategy first. Children in the observation condition solved the puzzle in an average of 3.1 minutes and made 4.2 errors, whereas children in the trial-and-error condition took 5.4 minutes and made 7.8 errors. The observation group was also more likely to use the demonstrated sequence of steps during later transfer tasks.
questions.stem: Which group solved the puzzle faster according to Figure 1?

choices.B: Children who observed an older peer first
choices.A: Children using trial and error only
choices.C: Both groups solved at the same speed
choices.D: The figure does not include solution time

explanations.B: Correct. The observation group averaged 3.1 minutes, clearly faster than the 5.4-minute trial-and-error group. The figure therefore shows a speed advantage for observational learning.
explanations.A: The trial-and-error group took longer, not less time.
explanations.C: The groups differ meaningfully in average time.
explanations.D: Solution time is one of the displayed outcomes.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Mean puzzle-solution time and number of errors for children learning by observation versus trial and error.

questions.stem: Watching an older peer demonstrate a strategy most directly illustrates what concept?

explanations.C: Correct. Children in the observation condition learned from seeing another person demonstrate the sequence. That is a classic example of observational learning, often discussed in social learning theory.
explanations.A: Operant extinction involves weakening of a response when reinforcement stops.
explanations.B: Attachment security concerns caregiver-child socioemotional bonds.
explanations.D: The placebo effect concerns expectation-related changes in treatment contexts.

questions.stem: Why is the transfer-task behavior important?

choices.A: It suggests the children learned an underlying strategy rather than only memorizing one outcome
choices.B: It proves children in the other condition learned nothing at all
choices.C: It means the observed peer became their attachment figure
choices.D: It shows that making more errors is always beneficial

explanations.A: Correct. Using the demonstrated sequence in later tasks indicates that the children extracted a generalizable strategy. That makes the learning effect more meaningful than solving one puzzle by chance.
explanations.B: The comparison group may still have learned something, just less efficiently.
explanations.C: Peer demonstration in this context does not imply attachment formation.
explanations.D: The figure shows fewer errors in the more successful condition, not that more errors were beneficial.

questions.stem: A classroom teacher applying these findings would most likely do what?

choices.D: Incorporate peer demonstration and guided modeling when introducing new tasks
choices.A: Avoid showing examples so students remain completely independent
choices.B: Assume children learn only through solitary trial and error
choices.C: Use demonstrations only after students have fully mastered the task

explanations.D: Correct. Because observation improved speed, accuracy, and later strategic transfer, teachers can reasonably use demonstrations and guided modeling to support new skill acquisition.
explanations.A: The study suggests examples can help rather than hinder learning.
explanations.B: The observation group contradicts the claim that solitary trial and error is the only route to learning.
explanations.C: Demonstrations were helpful at the start of learning, not just after full mastery.

questions.stem: Which statement is most supported by the data?

choices.C: Observational learning improved average efficiency and strategy use in this puzzle task
choices.A: Observation guaranteed success for every child
choices.B: Trial and error is useless in all developmental settings
choices.D: The older peer’s age was irrelevant because all children learn identically

explanations.C: Correct. The findings support group-level advantages for the observation condition on multiple measures. They do not justify absolutes about every individual or every learning situation.
explanations.A: Group means do not guarantee that every child succeeded in exactly the same way.
explanations.B: Trial and error may still be useful in other contexts; the study only shows it was less efficient here.
explanations.D: The passage does not support the claim that all children learn identically.

questions.passage: A lifespan study compared adults ages 20–30, 45–55, and 70–80 on a symbol-matching task and a vocabulary test. Mean symbol-matching scores were 92, 84, and 71, respectively. Mean vocabulary scores were 73, 79, and 81. Researchers argued that processing speed and accumulated verbal knowledge can follow different trajectories across adulthood.
questions.stem: Which age group had the highest vocabulary score?

choices.D: All age groups had the same vocabulary score

explanations.B: Correct. The oldest group scored 81 on vocabulary, which is higher than 79 for the middle group and 73 for the youngest group. The figure therefore identifies the oldest group as highest on vocabulary.
explanations.A: The youngest group had the lowest, not the highest, vocabulary score.
explanations.C: The middle group was intermediate and not the top scorer.
explanations.D: The vocabulary scores differ across groups.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Mean processing-speed and vocabulary scores across three adult age groups.

questions.stem: What pattern does the symbol-matching task show?

choices.D: Average processing-speed performance declined with age
choices.A: Processing speed increased steadily with age
choices.C: Vocabulary and processing speed changed identically

explanations.D: Correct. Symbol-matching scores move downward from 92 to 84 to 71 across the age groups, indicating lower average processing speed in older groups.
explanations.A: The figure shows the opposite of this statement.
explanations.B: The scores do not stay constant.
explanations.C: Vocabulary rises while symbol matching falls, so the trends are not identical.

questions.stem: Why do the researchers compare symbol matching with vocabulary?

choices.A: To show that different cognitive abilities can age differently
choices.B: To prove aging affects only language
choices.C: Because vocabulary is a biological measure of muscle strength
choices.D: To show that all test scores should move in the same direction

explanations.A: Correct. The paired measures reveal a contrast: one ability declines while another remains strong or improves. That helps illustrate the distinction between fluid-like processing speed and crystallized verbal knowledge.
explanations.B: The passage does not claim that aging affects only language.
explanations.C: Vocabulary is a cognitive measure, not a muscle-strength measure.
explanations.D: The point is precisely that scores do not all move in the same direction.

questions.stem: An educator designing lifelong-learning programs for older adults would most likely conclude what?

choices.B: Instruction can build on strong verbal knowledge even if rapid processing tasks become harder
choices.A: Older adults should avoid all learning because processing speed declines
choices.C: Vocabulary is useless once processing speed changes
choices.D: Only the youngest adults can benefit from verbal instruction

explanations.B: Correct. Because vocabulary remains relatively strong in older groups, educational programs can connect new material to existing knowledge while being mindful of slower speed on rapid novel tasks.
explanations.A: The data do not suggest that older adults should avoid learning.
explanations.C: Vocabulary remains a relative strength rather than becoming useless.
explanations.D: The figure does not imply that only younger adults can benefit from instruction.

choices.C: Cognitive aging is multidimensional, with some abilities declining and others remaining stronger
choices.A: All cognition worsens at exactly the same rate
choices.B: Older adults outperform younger adults on every measure in the figure
choices.D: Age differences are too inconsistent to interpret

explanations.C: Correct. The central lesson of the figure is that one measure declines while another remains strong or increases. That supports a multidimensional view of cognitive aging.
explanations.A: The figure directly contradicts a uniform single-rate decline.
explanations.B: Older adults do not outperform younger adults on the symbol-matching task.
explanations.D: The pattern is coherent and interpretable rather than random.
